1: vayavo'u vnei-Yisrael kol-ha'edah midbar-Tzin bachodesh harishon vayeshev ha'am beKadesh vatamot sham Miriam vatikkaVer sham: 'the people of Israel came into the wilderness of Zin in the first month, and the people stayed in Kadesh. And Miriam died there and was buried there.' Miriam's death is reported in a single verse with no mourning record — in sharp contrast to Aaron's later death (vv28-29) and Moses's anticipated death (Deut 34). Miriam was the first of the three Exodus siblings to die, and Numbers places her death immediately before the water-crisis (vv2-13). Kadesh = holy/sacred; the place named for holiness is the site of both death and the sin at the waters.
2: velo-hayah mayim la'edah vayikahalu al-Moshe ve'al-Aharon: 'and there was no water for the congregation. And they assembled themselves against Moses and against Aaron.' Vayikahalu (assembled against) — from kahal (assembly), but used here with the preposition al (against): the assembly forms itself in opposition. The same pattern as Korah's rebellion (Numbers 16:3, 42). The repetition of wilderness murmuring at water-shortage (cf. Exodus 17:1-7 at Rephidim/Massah-Meribah) signals a pattern of unbelief across the generation.
3: vayarev ha'am im-Moshe vayomeru lemor: 'and the people quarreled with Moses and said.' Vayarev from rib (contend/quarrel) — the word that gives Meribah (Merivah) its name (v13). The people's specific lament: 'if only we had died when our brothers died before YHWH' — they prefer the deaths of Korah's rebellion as their own fate over the present thirst. An extraordinary statement of despair.
4: velama heve'tem et-kehal YHWH el-hamidbar hazeh lamut sham anachnu uve'irenu: 'why have you brought the assembly of YHWH into this wilderness to die here, both we and our livestock?' Kehal YHWH (the assembly of YHWH) — the people identify themselves as YHWH's assembly even as they rebel. The complaint mirrors Exodus 17 almost word for word, showing the wilderness generation's cyclical unbelief despite the earlier Rephidim miracle.
5: velama he'eletunu mimitzrayim lavi'enu el-hamakom hara hazeh: 'why have you made us come up out of Egypt to bring us to this evil place?' The rhetorical question that appears repeatedly in wilderness complaints: 'why Egypt?' The questioners define Egypt as good and the wilderness as evil — inverting the Exodus's entire theological framework. Makom hara hazeh (this evil place) — their evaluation of YHWH's chosen wilderness road.
6: vayavo Moshe ve-Aharon miphney hakahal el-petach ohel-mo'ed vayiplu al-pneihem: 'Moses and Aaron went from the presence of the assembly to the entrance of the tent of meeting and fell on their faces.' The response to crisis: prostration before the tabernacle entrance. YHWH's glory (kevod YHWH) appears to them — as in the post-Korah plague intercession (Numbers 17:10, Hebrew). The pattern: crisis → Moses/Aaron to the tent entrance → divine appearance.
7: vayedaber YHWH el-Moshe lemor: YHWH speaks. The divine response to the water crisis is immediate. The instruction that follows is clear and specific — distinguishing this episode from Rephidim (Exod 17) where Moses was told to strike the rock.
8: kach et-hamateh vehakel et-ha'edah atah ve-Aharon achicha vedibertem el-hassela le'eineihem venatan memaiv: 'take the staff, and assemble the congregation, you and Aaron your brother, and tell [diber] the rock before their eyes to yield its water.' Two commands: take the staff and speak to the rock (daber, not naka = strike). The rock of Rephidim had to be struck (Exodus 17:6); this rock is to be addressed by word. The shift from striking to speaking reflects matured covenant communication — the word is sufficient.
9: vayikach Moshe et-hamateh milifney YHWH ka'asher tzivahu: 'Moses took the staff from before YHWH, as he commanded him.' Moses obeys the first part of the command — he takes the staff. The staff (matteh) from before YHWH is likely Aaron's budding rod (Numbers 17:10: 'put back the staff of Aaron before the testimony, to be kept as a sign'). Moses carries the staff of confirmed divine authority into the moment of crisis.
10: vayakehilu Moshe ve-Aharon et-hakahal el-penei hassela vayomer lahem shim'u-na hamoriyim: 'Moses and Aaron gathered the assembly together before the rock, and he said to them: Hear now, you rebels.' Hamoriyim — 'rebels' (from marah, to rebel/be bitter). This is Moses addressing the people — but the anger is his, not YHWH's command. The tone has shifted from faithful intercession (v6: falling on his face) to confrontational accusation.
11: vayarem Moshe et-yado vayak et-hassela bemattehu pa'amayim vayetze mayim rabbim vatesh ha'edah uve'ireyhem: 'Moses lifted up his hand and struck the rock with his staff twice, and water came out abundantly, and the congregation drank, and their livestock.' Moses strikes the rock — not once but twice. YHWH's command was to speak; Moses strikes. Water comes out abundantly (mayim rabbim) — the miracle occurs regardless of Moses's disobedience, but the sin has been committed. The abundance of water makes the disobedience more poignant: YHWH provides despite the failure of his servant.
12: vayomer YHWH el-Moshe ve'el-Aharon yaan lo-he'emantem bi lehaqdisheni le'einei vnei Yisrael lachen lo tavi'u et-hakahal hazeh el-ha'aretz asher natati lahem: 'YHWH said to Moses and Aaron: Because you did not believe in me, to uphold me as holy in the eyes of the people of Israel, therefore you shall not bring this assembly into the land that I have given them.' The sentence is severe and its grounds are specific: lo he'emantem bi (you did not believe/trust in me) + lehaqdisheni (to sanctify me/make me holy before them). The sin was not merely procedural disobedience but a failure of trust and a failure to demonstrate YHWH's holiness. He'emin (believe/trust) — the same root as amen; kadesh (sanctify) — the same root as holiness/sanctuary. The two failures are theological, not merely ritual.
13: hemah mei merivah asher-ravu vnei-Yisrael et-YHWH vayikadesh bam: 'these are the waters of Meribah, where the people of Israel quarreled with YHWH, and through them he showed himself holy.' Mei Merivah (waters of Meribah/quarreling): the place-name encodes Israel's sin. Vayikadesh bam: 'he showed himself holy through them' — despite and through the failure, YHWH's holiness was demonstrated. The holy name was not ultimately profaned; Moses's failure became the very occasion for YHWH's holiness to be visible.
14: vayishlach Moshe mal'achim miKadesh el-melech Edom: 'Moses sent messengers from Kadesh to the king of Edom.' Edom is Israel's brother-nation — Esau's descendants. The message opens with koh amar achyicha Yisrael (thus says your brother Israel): covenant kinship is invoked. The request for passage through the derech hamelech (King's Highway) — the major north-south trade route east of the Jordan.
15: veyede'u et asher asu-lanu mitzrayim: 'you know all the hardship that has come upon us, how our fathers went down to Egypt.' Moses's diplomatic speech invokes shared historical memory — the Egyptian sojourn affects both Israel and Edom as descendants of Jacob/Esau. The appeal is fraternal.
16: vayishma YHWH bekoleinu vayishlach malach vayotzi'enu mimitzrayim: 'YHWH heard our voice and sent an angel and brought us out of Egypt.' The Exodus summary in diplomatic language: YHWH heard, sent an angel (malach), brought out. The malach of the Exodus is a recurring figure (Exodus 14:19; Numbers 23:20; Numbers 33:2) whose identity is deeply debated — the Angel of YHWH who is identified with YHWH himself.
17: na ne'evrah be'artzecha lo na'avor besadeh uvekerem velo nishteh mei be'er derech hamelech nelech lo nitteh yamin usmol: 'please let us pass through your land. We will not pass through field or vineyard, or drink water from a well. We will go along the King's Highway. We will not turn aside to the right hand or to the left.' Derech hamelech (King's Highway) — the major international route; Israel promises to stay on it, taking nothing. The diplomatic protocol is correct; the request is reasonable.
18: vayomer elav Edom lo ta'avor bi pen-becharev etze likratecha: 'but Edom said to him: You shall not pass through, lest I come out with the sword against you.' Edom refuses. The fraternal appeal fails. The hostility between Israel and Edom — rooted in the Jacob/Esau rivalry (Gen 27) — persists through the wilderness period. The prophets (Obadiah; Isa 34; Amos 1:11) later pronounce judgment on Edom's persistent hostility to Jacob's descendants.
19: vayomru elav bnei Yisrael bamesila na'aleh ve'im-memeiv nishteh ani uve'iruy venatati michram vrak ein-davar beragli e'evorah: Israel offers to pay for any water drunk and guarantees it is only a matter of passing through on foot. The repeated protest of harmless passage — beragli e'evorah (on foot I will pass through) — emphasizes Israel's peaceful intent.
20: vayomer lo ta'avor vayetze Edom likrato be'am kaved uved-chazak: 'but he said: You shall not pass through. And Edom came out against them with a large army and with a strong force.' Am kaved uved chazak (heavy people and strong hand) — military terms. Israel turns aside. The road through Edom is blocked; Israel will have to go around, adding distance and hardship to the wilderness journey.
21: vayema'en Edom netet et-Yisrael avor bigvulo vayyet Yisrael me'alav: 'Edom refused to give Israel passage through his territory, so Israel turned away from him.' Vayma'en (refused) — Edom's repeated refusal. Israel turns away (vayyet) — literally 'veered away.' The word anticipates the later wilderness journey narrative where veering away from YHWH's path is a constant temptation.
22: vayisu miKadesh vayavo'u vnei Yisrael kol-ha'edah hor hahar: 'and they journeyed from Kadesh, and the people of Israel, the whole congregation, came to Mount Hor.' Hor hahar (Mount Hor) — a place name meaning literally 'the mountain of the mountain' or 'peak mountain,' location uncertain (traditionally identified with Jabal Harun near Petra). The next major event is Aaron's death.
23: vayomer YHWH el-Moshe ve'el-Aharon behor hahar al-gevul eretz-Edom lemor: YHWH speaks at the Edom border. The connection between Edom's refusal and Aaron's death is temporal: the rejected passage and the dying of the old generation continue in sequence.
24: ye'asef Aharon el-amav ki lo yavo el-ha'aretz asher natati livnei Yisrael yaan asher meritem et-pi lemei merivah: 'Aaron shall be gathered to his people, for he shall not enter the land that I have given to the people of Israel, because you rebelled against my command at the waters of Meribah.' Ye'asef (shall be gathered) — the nif'al passive: Aaron is gathered; his death is a being-taken. El-amav (to his people): the patriarchal death formula (cf. Genesis 25:8 Abraham; 35:29 Isaac) — covenant belonging continues beyond death. The reason for Aaron's death outside the land is traced explicitly to Merivah (asher meritem et-pi: you rebelled against my mouth/command).
25: kach et-Aharon ve'et-Eleazar beno veha'el otam hor hahar: 'take Aaron and Eleazar his son and bring them up to Mount Hor.' The three-generation ascent: Moses leads Aaron and Eleazar up the mountain. The father-son priestly succession is embedded in the death narrative.
26: ufashat et-Aharon et-begadav vehalbashet otam et-Eleazar beno veAharon ye'asef umet sham: 'and strip Aaron of his garments and put them on Eleazar his son. And Aaron shall be gathered to his people and shall die there.' The transfer of the high-priestly garments from Aaron to Eleazar is the transfer of the priesthood itself. Ufashat... vehalbashet (strip... and put on): the investiture of Eleazar is simultaneously the divestiture of Aaron. The high-priestly office cannot die; it passes from father to son in the moment of death. Hebrews 7:23-24: the Levitical priests 'were prevented by death from continuing in office; but he [Christ] holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever.'
27: vaya'as Moshe ka'asher tzivah YHWH vaya'alu el-hor hahar le'einei kol-ha'edah: 'Moses did as YHWH commanded. They went up to Mount Hor in the sight of all the congregation.' Le'einei kol-ha'edah (in the sight of all the congregation) — the succession is public, visible to the whole community. The priestly transition is not private or secret; the entire assembly witnesses Aaron's death and Eleazar's installation.
28: vayifashet Moshe et-Aharon et-begadav vayalbes otam et-Eleazar beno vayamat Aharon sham bero'sh hahar: 'Moses stripped Aaron of his garments and put them on Eleazar his son. And Aaron died there on the top of the mountain.' Aaron dies clothed in the priestly absence of his garments — having given them to the next generation. The death of Aaron at the mountain top: naked of his office, yet within it. He dies as the priestly generation passes; the office lives in Eleazar.
29: vayir'u kol-ha'edah ki gava Aharon vayivku et-Aharon shloshim yom kol beit Yisrael: 'when all the congregation saw that Aaron had breathed his last, all the house of Israel wept for Aaron thirty days.' Gava (breathed his last/expired) — the dying verb; vayivku (wept). Thirty days of mourning — the same as Moses will receive (Deuteronomy 34:8). The full-community mourning for Aaron stands in sharp contrast to the one-sentence burial of Miriam (v1). Aaron's death is communally mourned; he was the beloved high priest who stood between the living and the dead (Numbers 16:48).
